title: Fluids and Blood Transfusion MCQ – Chapter 7
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: According to the textbook, fluids are broadly divided into which two categories?

options.B: Crystalloids and colloids

questions.explanation: Fluids are divided into crystalloids and colloids as stated at the beginning of Chapter 7.

questions.question: What is the composition of Plasmalyte solution as per the textbook?

options.B: Na+ 140, Cl- 98, K+ 3, Mg2+ 3, Acetate 27, Gluconate 23 mEq/L

questions.explanation: Plasmalyte composition: Na+ 140, Cl- 98, K+ 3, Mg2+ 3, Acetate 27, Gluconate 23 mEq/L. Acetate and gluconate are metabolized to generate bicarbonate.

questions.question: Normal saline is preferred over Ringer lactate in all of the following conditions EXCEPT:

options.B: Brain injury

questions.explanation: As per the textbook, NS is preferred over RL for treating: hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, brain injury (Ca2+ in RL can increase neuronal injury), and hyponatremia.

questions.question: According to Table 7.1, crystalloids should be replaced in what ratio to blood loss?

options.B: 1.5:1 ratio (150 mL crystalloid for 100 mL blood loss)

questions.explanation: As per Table 7.1, crystalloids should be replaced in a ratio of 1.5:1. For example, blood loss of 100 mL should be replaced with 150 mL of crystalloid.

questions.question: Why should blood NOT be given through the same drip set as Ringer lactate?

options.B: RL contains calcium which can cause clotting of blood
options.C: RL is hypertonic and can lyse RBCs
options.D: RL contains potassium which causes hemolysis

questions.explanation: As RL contains calcium, blood should not be given through the same drip set as calcium can initiate coagulation of stored blood.

questions.question: Which of the following is an isotonic crystalloid solution?

options.B: Normal saline (0.9% NaCl)

questions.explanation: Normal saline (0.9% NaCl) is an isotonic crystalloid with an osmolarity of 308 mOsm/L, close to plasma osmolarity.

questions.question: The sodium content of normal saline (0.9% NaCl) is:

options.B: 130 mEq/L

questions.explanation: Normal saline contains 154 mEq/L of sodium and 154 mEq/L of chloride.

questions.question: Ringer's lactate solution contains all of the following EXCEPT:

options.B: K+ 5 mEq/L

questions.explanation: Ringer's lactate contains Na+ 131, Cl- 111, K+ 5, Ca2+ 2, Lactate 29 mEq/L, pH 6.5. It does not contain bicarbonate directly.

questions.question: The lactate in Ringer's lactate is converted to bicarbonate in the:

options.B: Liver

questions.explanation: Lactate is metabolized by the liver to bicarbonate, providing a buffering effect.

questions.question: Excessive administration of normal saline can cause:

options.B: Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis

questions.explanation: Large volumes of NS (high chloride 154 mEq/L) can cause dilutional hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.

questions.question: 5% Dextrose is considered isotonic in the bag but acts as a hypotonic solution in vivo because:

options.B: Dextrose is rapidly metabolized, leaving free water that distributes across all compartments

questions.explanation: After infusion, glucose is rapidly metabolized, leaving free water that distributes evenly across ICF and ECF, effectively acting as a hypotonic solution.

questions.question: What fraction of infused crystalloid remains in the intravascular space after 1 hour?

options.B: About 75%

questions.explanation: Only about 25% of infused isotonic crystalloid remains intravascularly; the rest redistributes to the interstitial space.

questions.question: Colloids differ from crystalloids in that they:

options.A: Are freely filtered across capillary membranes
options.B: Contain large molecular weight substances that maintain oncotic pressure and stay intravascular longer
options.D: Cannot be used for volume resuscitation

questions.explanation: Colloids contain large molecules (proteins/starches) that generate oncotic pressure, keeping fluid in the intravascular space longer than crystalloids.

questions.question: Which of the following is a natural colloid?

options.B: Dextran

questions.explanation: Human albumin is the only natural colloid. HES, dextrans, and gelatins are synthetic colloids.

questions.question: The approximate intravascular half-life of infused colloids compared to crystalloids is:

options.B: Same

questions.explanation: Colloids have a longer intravascular half-life (hours) compared to crystalloids because large molecules are retained within the vascular space.

questions.question: Dextran 40 is used primarily for:

options.B: Improving microcirculation and reducing blood viscosity (rheological effects)

questions.explanation: Dextran 40 (low molecular weight) improves microcirculation by reducing blood viscosity and preventing rouleaux formation.

questions.question: A major side effect of dextran infusion is:

options.B: Anaphylactic reactions and interference with blood cross-matching

questions.explanation: Dextrans can cause anaphylaxis, interfere with blood cross-matching (rouleaux formation), and impair coagulation at high doses.

questions.question: Hydroxyethyl starch (HES) solutions can cause:

options.B: Coagulopathy and renal impairment at high doses

questions.explanation: HES can cause dose-dependent coagulopathy (reduced Factor VIII, von Willebrand factor) and nephrotoxicity, especially in critically ill patients.

questions.question: Gelatin-based colloids (e.g., Haemaccel) have which disadvantage?

options.B: Risk of anaphylactoid reactions and relatively short duration of action

questions.explanation: Gelatins have a shorter duration than other colloids and carry a risk of anaphylactoid/allergic reactions.

questions.question: The daily maintenance fluid requirement for a 70 kg adult using the 4-2-1 rule is approximately:

options.B: 110 mL/hr

questions.explanation: 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/hr for first 10 kg (40) + 2 mL/kg/hr for next 10 kg (20) + 1 mL/kg/hr for remaining 50 kg (50) = 110 mL/hr.

questions.question: The 4-2-1 rule for hourly maintenance fluid calculation gives 4 mL/kg/hr for:

options.B: First 10 kg

questions.explanation: The 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/hr for the first 10 kg, 2 mL/kg/hr for the next 10 kg, and 1 mL/kg/hr for each kg above 20 kg.

questions.question: Intraoperative fluid therapy must account for:

options.B: Maintenance, deficit (fasting), and ongoing losses (surgical/third space)

questions.explanation: Intraoperative fluids must replace maintenance needs, preoperative fasting deficit, and ongoing surgical/third-space losses.

questions.question: Third-space fluid loss refers to:

options.A: Blood loss into the suction canister
options.B: Functional loss of fluid into a non-equilibrating space (e.g., bowel wall, traumatized tissues)

questions.explanation: Third-space loss is the redistribution of fluid into a non-functional compartment (traumatized tissue, bowel wall, peritoneal cavity) that does not participate in normal fluid exchange.

questions.question: For major abdominal surgery, the estimated third-space fluid loss is approximately:

options.B: 4–8 mL/kg/hr

questions.explanation: Third-space losses vary: 1–2 mL/kg/hr for minimal trauma, 4–6 for moderate, and 6–10 mL/kg/hr for major abdominal surgery.

questions.question: The preoperative fasting deficit is calculated as:

options.B: Hourly maintenance rate × number of hours NPO

questions.explanation: Fasting deficit = maintenance fluid rate (by 4-2-1 rule) × hours of fasting (NPO period).

questions.question: Whole blood transfusion contains all components of blood and has a hematocrit of approximately:
questions.explanation: Whole blood has a hematocrit of approximately 35–40% and contains RBCs, plasma, platelets, and clotting factors.

questions.question: Packed red blood cells (PRBCs) have a hematocrit of approximately:
questions.explanation: PRBCs have a hematocrit of 55–70% after removal of most plasma, concentrating the red cells.

questions.question: One unit of packed RBCs raises hemoglobin by approximately:

options.B: 1 g/dL

questions.explanation: One unit of PRBCs raises hemoglobin by approximately 1 g/dL (or hematocrit by 3%) in an average adult.

questions.question: The storage temperature for packed red blood cells is:
questions.explanation: PRBCs are stored at 1–6°C with a shelf life of 35–42 days depending on the anticoagulant/preservative used.

questions.question: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is indicated for:

options.B: Replacement of coagulation factors in patients with coagulopathy

questions.explanation: FFP contains all coagulation factors and is indicated for coagulopathy, DIC, massive transfusion, and warfarin reversal.

questions.question: Platelet concentrate transfusion is indicated when platelet count falls below:
questions.explanation: Platelet transfusion is generally indicated when count is <50,000/μL or <75,000–100,000/μL with active bleeding or before surgery.

questions.question: Platelets are stored at:

options.B: 20–24°C with continuous agitation

questions.explanation: Platelets are stored at room temperature (20–24°C) with continuous gentle agitation, with a shelf life of 5 days.

questions.question: The most common cause of fatal transfusion reaction is:

options.B: ABO-incompatible (hemolytic) transfusion due to clerical error

questions.explanation: ABO-incompatible acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, usually due to clerical/identification error, is the most common cause of fatal transfusion reactions.

questions.question: Signs of acute hemolytic transfusion reaction under general anesthesia include:

options.B: Unexplained hypotension, fever, hemoglobinuria, and DIC

questions.explanation: Under GA, signs include unexplained hypotension, tachycardia, fever, hemoglobinuria (dark urine), diffuse bleeding (DIC), and bronchospasm.

questions.question: The first step in managing suspected hemolytic transfusion reaction is:

options.B: Stop the transfusion immediately and maintain IV access
options.C: Administer more fluids through the same line

questions.explanation: The immediate action is to STOP the transfusion, maintain IV access with new tubing, and send the blood bag and patient sample for verification.

questions.question: Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) presents with:

options.B: Acute respiratory distress, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hours of transfusion

questions.explanation: TRALI presents with acute hypoxemia, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, and respiratory distress within 6 hours of transfusion, without circulatory overload.

questions.question: Massive blood transfusion is defined as replacement of:

options.B: More than one blood volume (or >10 units PRBCs) in 24 hours

questions.explanation: Massive transfusion is defined as replacement of more than one blood volume (approximately 70 mL/kg or >10 units PRBCs) within 24 hours.

questions.question: Complications of massive blood transfusion include all EXCEPT:

options.B: Hyperkalemia

questions.explanation: Massive transfusion complications include hypothermia, hyperkalemia, citrate toxicity (hypocalcemia), coagulopathy, and acid-base disturbance – not polycythemia.

questions.question: Citrate toxicity from massive transfusion manifests as:

options.B: Hypocalcemia (ionized calcium reduction) causing cardiac depression

questions.explanation: Citrate (anticoagulant in stored blood) chelates ionized calcium, causing hypocalcemia which can lead to cardiac depression and coagulopathy.

questions.question: Hyperkalemia in stored blood occurs because:

options.B: Red cell membrane Na-K ATPase pump fails during storage, leaking potassium into plasma

questions.explanation: During storage, the RBC membrane Na-K ATPase pump becomes inactive, causing potassium to leak from cells into the plasma of stored blood.

questions.question: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction is caused by:

options.B: Antibodies against donor leukocyte antigens or cytokines in stored blood

questions.explanation: Febrile non-hemolytic reactions are caused by recipient antibodies against donor WBC antigens or accumulated cytokines in stored blood components.

questions.question: Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO) is characterized by:

options.B: Pulmonary edema, hypertension, dyspnea due to volume overload

questions.explanation: TACO presents with signs of circulatory overload: pulmonary edema, raised JVP, hypertension, and dyspnea, especially in elderly and cardiac patients.

questions.question: Infectious complications of blood transfusion include all EXCEPT:

options.B: HIV

questions.explanation: Transfusion-transmitted infections include hepatitis B, C, HIV, malaria, syphilis, and CMV. TB is not a common transfusion-transmitted infection.

questions.question: Autologous blood transfusion refers to:

options.A: Transfusing blood from a family member
options.B: Transfusing the patient's own previously collected or salvaged blood

questions.explanation: Autologous transfusion involves collecting, storing, and re-infusing the patient's own blood, eliminating risks of alloimmunization and transfusion-transmitted infections.

questions.question: Types of autologous blood transfusion include all EXCEPT:

options.B: Acute normovolemic hemodilution (ANH)

questions.explanation: Autologous methods include preoperative donation, acute normovolemic hemodilution, and intraoperative cell salvage. Emergency crossmatch uses donor (allogeneic) blood.

questions.question: In acute normovolemic hemodilution (ANH), blood is removed preoperatively and replaced with:

options.B: Crystalloids or colloids to maintain normovolemia

questions.explanation: In ANH, blood is withdrawn immediately before surgery and replaced with crystalloid/colloid to maintain intravascular volume. The blood is re-infused after surgical bleeding.

questions.question: Intraoperative cell salvage is contraindicated in:

options.B: Surgery for malignancy (relative) and infected surgical fields

questions.explanation: Cell salvage is relatively contraindicated in malignancy (risk of tumor cell dissemination) and contaminated/infected surgical fields.

questions.question: The trigger for blood transfusion in a healthy patient is generally a hemoglobin of:

options.B: 7–8 g/dL

questions.explanation: In otherwise healthy patients, transfusion is generally triggered at hemoglobin 7–8 g/dL (restrictive strategy). Higher thresholds apply for cardiac disease.

questions.question: The anticoagulant used in blood storage bags is:

options.B: CPDA-1 (Citrate-Phosphate-Dextrose-Adenine)

questions.explanation: CPDA-1 is the standard anticoagulant-preservative used in blood bags. Citrate prevents coagulation, phosphate and dextrose support RBC metabolism, and adenine maintains ATP levels.

questions.question: Blood must be transfused through a filter of pore size:

options.B: 170–260 micrometers (standard blood filter)

questions.explanation: Standard blood transfusion sets have a 170–260 micrometer filter to remove clots and debris. Microaggregate filters (20–40 μm) are used for special indications.
